The **N Broadway Express**[[3]](#cite_note-timetable-3) is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") service in the [B Division](/wiki/B_Division_(New_York_City_Subway) "B Division (New York City Subway)") of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"). Its route emblem, or "bullet," is colored yellow, since it uses the [BMT Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The N operates 24 hours daily between [Ditmars Boulevard](/wiki/Astoria%E2%80%93Ditmars_Boulevard_(BMT_Astoria_Line) "Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard (BMT Astoria Line)") in [Astoria, Queens](/wiki/Astoria,_Queens "Astoria, Queens"), and [Stillwell Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_(BMT_Sea_Beach_Line) "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue (BMT Sea Beach Line)") in [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"); limited rush hour service originates and terminates at [96th Street](/wiki/96th_Street_(Second_Avenue_Subway) "96th Street (Second Avenue Subway)") on the [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side") of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") instead of Ditmars Boulevard.[[5]](#cite_note-2AVS-5)[[3]](#cite_note-timetable-3)[[6]](#cite_note-2AVN-6) Weekday daytime service makes express stops between [34th Street–Herald Square](/wiki/34th_Street%E2%80%93Herald_Square_station#BMT_Broadway_Line_platforms "34th Street–Herald Square station") in Manhattan and [59th Street](/wiki/59th_Street_station_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "59th Street station (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and all stops elsewhere. Weekend daytime service is the same as weekday daytime service, except trains make all stops in Manhattan between 34th and [Canal Street](/wiki/Canal_Street_station_(New_York_City_Subway) "Canal Street station (New York City Subway)"). Overnight service makes all stops along the full route, serves the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan") of [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") and uses the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel") to travel between Manhattan and Brooklyn instead of the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge").[[a]](#cite_note-manhattan-bridge-night-8)

The N was originally the [Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation")'s **4** service, running along the BMT Sea Beach Line to the Manhattan Bridge. The 4 used the [BMT Nassau Street Line](/wiki/BMT_Nassau_Street_Line "BMT Nassau Street Line") in Lower Manhattan from 1915 to 1917, after which it ran express on the BMT Broadway Line. The 4 became the N in 1961. The N ran local in Queens along the [IND Queens Boulevard Line](/wiki/IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line "IND Queens Boulevard Line") to [Forest Hills–71st Avenue](/wiki/Forest_Hills%E2%80%9371st_Avenue_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Forest Hills–71st Avenue (IND Queens Boulevard Line)") from 1976 until 1987, when it switched terminals with the R. From 1986 to 2004, reconstruction on the Manhattan Bridge forced the N to run local on the Broadway Line via the Montague Street Tunnel.

## Service history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=N_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Service history")]

### Before 1970

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=N_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Before 1970")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/NX_A_New_Service.gif/250px-NX_A_New_Service.gif)](/wiki/File:NX_A_New_Service.gif)

Poster advertising a new NX service

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/The_NX_joins_the_N.gif/250px-The_NX_joins_the_N.gif)](/wiki/File:The_NX_joins_the_N.gif)

Poster advertising the NX service's discontinuation

The route that is now the N was originally [BMT](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation") service 4, known as the *Sea Beach Line* or *Sea Beach Express*.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-9)

On June 22, 1915, the current [BMT Sea Beach Line](/wiki/BMT_Sea_Beach_Line "BMT Sea Beach Line") opened, replacing a street level "el" that branched off of the [Fifth Avenue El](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue_Line_(Brooklyn_elevated) "Fifth Avenue Line (Brooklyn elevated)") with the former [BMT West End Line](/wiki/BMT_West_End_Line "BMT West End Line"). Originally, it used the south tracks of the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), which at that time connected to the [BMT Nassau Street Line](/wiki/BMT_Nassau_Street_Line "BMT Nassau Street Line").[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)[[10]](#cite_note-:03-11) Trains began running express between Pacific Street and 59th Street with the extension of the Fourth Avenue Line to 86th Street.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

On September 4, 1917, the first part of the [BMT Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") and the north side tracks of the Manhattan Bridge opened. Trains ran from [14th Street–Union Square](/wiki/14th_Street%E2%80%93Union_Square_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "14th Street–Union Square (BMT Broadway Line)") to [Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_(BMT_Sea_Beach_Line) "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue (BMT Sea Beach Line)"), now using the bridge's northern tracks.[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)[[12]](#cite_note-13) On January 15, 1918, service was extended to [Times Square–42nd Street](/wiki/Times_Square%E2%80%9342nd_Street_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "Times Square–42nd Street (BMT Broadway Line)").[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

Weekday and Saturday summer trains leaving Coney Island between 6:37 a.m. and 8:37 p.m. began running non-stop between Kings Highway and 59th Street on August 1, 1920. Trains that started at Kings Highway made local stops. On November 14, 1920, weekday rush hour trains, and Saturday AM rush hour trains began skipping Myrtle Avenue and DeKalb Avenue.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

Between 1924 and 1926, trains skipped Myrtle Avenue and DeKalb Avenue during weekday rush hours, and on Saturday southbound between 12:01 p.m. and 6:57 p.m. Between 4:30 p.m. and 7:30 p.m. weekdays, and Saturdays southbound between 12:01 p.m. to 6:57 p.m., the rear two cars at Canal Street were restricted to boarding only (no exiting the train). In June 1931, trains skipped Myrtle Avenue and DeKalb Avenue between 7 a.m. and 12:30 a.m. weekdays, and between 10:30 a.m. Sunday to 12:30 a.m. Monday. Trains skipped 36th Street during the morning rush hour in the northbound direction. Between 1933 and 1937, the hours in which trains skipped Myrtle Avenue and DeKalb Avenue were 7 a.m. until midnight weekdays.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

On June 29, 1950, trains began running express in Brooklyn during late nights. As of June 28, 1951, every other morning rush hour train no longer terminated at Kings Highway.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

On May 2, 1957, service was extended north via the express tracks from Times Square to [57th Street–Seventh Avenue](/wiki/57th_Street%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "57th Street–Seventh Avenue (BMT Broadway Line)"), and trains stopped bypassing 36th Street during the AM rush hour. In fall 1959, trains began stopping at [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "DeKalb Avenue (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") during midday hours. Previously, they bypassed DeKalb Avenue at all times except late nights. Beginning on January 1, 1961, trains bypassed [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "DeKalb Avenue (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") during rush hours only. In addition, on weekday and Saturday evenings, late nights, and all day Sundays, they ran local on the BMT Fourth Avenue Line.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

The N designation began to appear when [R27](/wiki/R27_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R27 (New York City Subway car)") subway cars were moved to the service in April 1961.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)

The NX designation was used for a [rush hour](/wiki/Rush_hour "Rush hour") peak-direction "super-express" service along the express tracks of the Sea Beach Line, beginning in the AM rush hour at [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Brighton Beach (BMT Brighton Line)") on the [BMT Brighton Line](/wiki/BMT_Brighton_Line "BMT Brighton Line"), running through Coney Island, and then following the N route to [57th Street–Seventh Avenue](/wiki/57th_Street%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "57th Street–Seventh Avenue (BMT Broadway Line)"). (Reverse in the PM rush hour.) This short-lived service began on November 27, 1967 (with the opening of the [Chrystie Street Connection](/wiki/Chrystie_Street_Connection "Chrystie Street Connection"))[[13]](#cite_note-14) and ended April 12, 1968, due to low ridership. Starting on Monday, April 15, 1968, the five NX trips instead ran as N trips.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

On December 31, 1972, all late night trains began running local in Brooklyn. Late weekday evening trains, weekend southbound trains, and northbound early weekday trains began stopping at 53rd Street and 45th Street.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

### 1970–1980

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=N_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1970–1980")]

On August 30, 1976, weekday N service was extended north over the BMT [60th Street Tunnel Connection](/wiki/60th_Street_Tunnel_Connection "60th Street Tunnel Connection") to [Forest Hills–71st Avenue](/wiki/Forest_Hills%E2%80%9371st_Avenue_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Forest Hills–71st Avenue (IND Queens Boulevard Line)") to replace the discontinued [EE](/wiki/EE_(Broadway_Local) "EE (Broadway Local)"). While many N trains ran the full route from Coney Island to 71st Avenue, via the Manhattan Bridge and Broadway Express, some trains ran local during the rush hours only (southbound in the AM rush hour, and northbound in the PM rush hour) between [Whitehall Street–South Ferry](/wiki/Whitehall_Street%E2%80%93South_Ferry_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "Whitehall Street–South Ferry (BMT Broadway Line)") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") and [Forest Hills–71st Avenue](/wiki/Forest_Hills%E2%80%9371st_Avenue_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Forest Hills–71st Avenue (IND Queens Boulevard Line)"), which had been the former EE route;[[15]](#cite_note-16) these trains were noted with diamond N bullets from 1979. Trains stopped terminating at Kings Highway in the AM rush hour.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

On January 24, 1977, as part of a series of NYCTA service cuts to save $13 million, many subway lines began running shorter trains during middays. As part of the change, N trains began running with four cars between 9:30 a.m. and 2:15 p.m.[[16]](#cite_note-17) On August 27, 1977, N service was cut back during late nights, only operating between [36th Street](/wiki/36th_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "36th Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") and [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_(New_York_City_Subway) "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue (New York City Subway)").[[17]](#cite_note-18) Northbound trains ran express between 59th Street and 36th Street while southbound trains ran local.[[11]](#cite_note-:5-12)

### 1980–1990

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=N_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980–1990")]

On November 26, 1984, evening rush hour trains that terminated at Kings Highway were extended to Coney Island.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19)

Reconstruction of the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") between 1986 and 2004 disrupted N service, usually rerouting it via the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel"). On April 26, 1986, the north side tracks (leading to the [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line")) were closed and services that normally ran on them were moved to the south side, running via the [BMT Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line"). Because of the large amount of train traffic now running on those tracks, rush hour and midday N service was rerouted via the Montague Street Tunnel, making local stops in Manhattan and Brooklyn, though evening, night and weekend trains continued to use the bridge and express tracks in Brooklyn.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10) Late evening trains that ran via the bridge also ran local in Brooklyn heading southbound. Six trains only ran between Continental Avenue and Whitehall Street in the AM rush hour, while six trains only ran between Canal Street and Continental Avenue in the PM rush hour.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19) The [M](/wiki/M_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "M (New York City Subway service)"), which was rerouted from the [BMT Brighton Line](/wiki/BMT_Brighton_Line "BMT Brighton Line") to the [BMT West End Line](/wiki/BMT_West_End_Line "BMT West End Line"), replaced the N as the weekday express on the Fourth Avenue Line.[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)[[19]](#cite_note-20) Starting on April 28, 1986, one Q train ran to Continental Avenue at 7:05 a.m., and returned in service as an N train, and an N train arriving at Continental Avenue at 3:25 p.m. began returning in service as a B train. These special B and Q trips no longer ran after May 23, 1987.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19)

As part of the New York City Transit Authority's proposed service plan to serve the new Archer Avenue Line upper level, the N would have been extended to Jamaica Center during weekdays. When N trains terminated at 71st Avenue or [57th Street–Seventh Avenue](/wiki/57th_Street%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_station "57th Street–Seventh Avenue station") during weekends and evenings, G trains would have been extended to Jamaica Center, and during late nights a G train shuttle would have run between Jamaica Center and [Van Wyck Boulevard](/wiki/Briarwood_station "Briarwood station"). This service plan would have allowed E and F trains to remain on the Queens Boulevard mainline toward 179th Street.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23) The final service plan, which took effect on December 11, 1988, had the extension served by E trains, with R trains extended to 179th Street.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

On May 24, 1987, the N swapped northern terminals with the [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)"). The N was switched to [Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard](/wiki/Astoria%E2%80%93Ditmars_Boulevard_(BMT_Astoria_Line) "Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard (BMT Astoria Line)"), while the [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") went to [Forest Hills–71st Avenue](/wiki/Forest_Hills%E2%80%9371st_Avenue_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Forest Hills–71st Avenue (IND Queens Boulevard Line)").[[24]](#cite_note-:3-25): 16 This was done to give the R direct access to [Jamaica Yard](/wiki/Jamaica_Yard "Jamaica Yard"); previously, the N had direct access to both [Jamaica Yard](/wiki/Jamaica_Yard "Jamaica Yard") and [Coney Island Yard](/wiki/Coney_Island_Complex "Coney Island Complex"), and the R, running from Bay Ridge to Astoria, lacked direct access to any yard.[[25]](#cite_note-26) This change was intended to improve the appearance and reliability of service on the R, since all trains on the Astoria and Broadway Lines were part of the graffiti-free program.[[24]](#cite_note-:3-25): 16 Trains ran via the Manhattan Bridge during late nights and weekends. Some trains went into service at 86th Street during the AM rush hour put-ins.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19) Between November 23, 1987, and May 13, 1988, one D train arrived at Ditmars Boulevard at 7:12 a.m. and returned in service as an N train.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19)

When the north side of the Manhattan Bridge reopened and the south side was closed on December 11, 1988, the N began running local in Manhattan and via the Montague Tunnel at all times. In order to replace B service[[b]](#cite_note-28) to Ditmars Boulevard, additional N service was provided during rush hours. Trains continued to run express in Brooklyn between [Pacific Street](/wiki/Pacific_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "Pacific Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") and [59th Street/Fourth Avenue](/wiki/59th_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "59th Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") evenings and weekends.[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19)

On June 11, 1990, trains that were put into service at Queensboro Plaza in the PM rush hour were discontinued. Between June 10, 1990, and July 25, 1992, due to construction work in the 60th Street Tunnel, trains had to use a single track. Trains operated either between Ditmars Boulevard and Canal Street, or between 57th Street and Coney Island.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19)

The Transit Authority and politicians pressured the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") to resume N train service on the bridge's south side on September 30, 1990, despite warnings from engineers that the structure was unsafe and major repairs still had to be made. Trains ran express on Broadway in Manhattan (stopping at 49th Street) and Fourth Avenue in Brooklyn at all times except late nights (10 p.m. to 5 a.m.). Southbound Brooklyn expresses ran until late evenings on Saturday, not running on Sunday morning any more. Between early morning and early evenings, trains skipped DeKalb Avenue. Several trains after the AM rush hour went out of service at Kings Highway.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19) On December 27, state inspectors forced N service to be rerouted via the Montague Street Tunnel again after discovery of corroded support beams and missing steel plates, running local on its entire route at all times.[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)

### 1990–2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=N_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990–2000")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/R68_2880_on_the_N.jpg/250px-R68_2880_on_the_N.jpg)](/wiki/File:R68_2880_on_the_N.jpg)

An N train of [R68s](/wiki/R68_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R68 (New York City Subway car)") at [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_station_(BMT_Astoria_Line) "Broadway station (BMT Astoria Line)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/MTA_NYC_Subway_N_train_arriving_at_36th_Ave.jpg/250px-MTA_NYC_Subway_N_train_arriving_at_36th_Ave.jpg)](/wiki/File:MTA_NYC_Subway_N_train_arriving_at_36th_Ave.jpg)

An N train of [R68As](/wiki/R68A_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R68A (New York City Subway car)") at [36th Avenue](/wiki/36th_Avenue_station "36th Avenue station")

In November 1993, before the Manhattan Bridge's renovation was completed, the MTA proposed restoring express N service along the Fourth Avenue Line for a six-month trial period, with the M making local stops between [Pacific Street](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue%E2%80%93Barclays_Center_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") and [36th Street](/wiki/36th_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "36th Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)"). Some residents of [Sunset Park, Brooklyn](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn"), opposed the proposal, which they claimed would negatively impact about 7,000 daily riders at the [45th Street](/wiki/45th_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "45th Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") and [53rd Street](/wiki/53rd_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "53rd Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") stations.[[30]](#cite_note-32) On May 31, 1994, the N began running express in Brooklyn between Pacific Street and [59th Street–Fourth Avenue](/wiki/59th_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "59th Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") during weekday midday and rush hours, with the M running local during those times.[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)[[32]](#cite_note-34)
From 1994 to May 22, 1995, the southern terminal of the N was [86th Street](/wiki/86th_Street_(BMT_Sea_Beach_Line) "86th Street (BMT Sea Beach Line)") due to rehabilitation work at [Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_(BMT_Sea_Beach_Line) "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue (BMT Sea Beach Line)").[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36) On November 4, 2001, it was cut back again as the terminal's reconstruction project continued.[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)

From April 30, 1995, to November 12, 1995, the north side of the Manhattan Bridge was closed during weekday midday and weekends for structural repairs. Midday N service ran local in Brooklyn, replacing the M, which was cut back from [9th Avenue](/wiki/9th_Avenue_(BMT_West_End_Line) "9th Avenue (BMT West End Line)") to [Chambers Street](/wiki/Chambers_Street_(BMT_Nassau_Street_Line) "Chambers Street (BMT Nassau Street Line)"). The N continued to run express during rush hours.[[35]](#cite_note-37)[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-39)

### 2001–2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=N_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2001–2010")]

After the [September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") attacks, N service was initially rerouted between Canal Street and Atlantic Avenue–Pacific Street and operated via the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), making all stops along the full route, but was split on the evening of September 12, with the northern section operating between Ditmars Boulevard and 34th Street, and the southern section operating between Court Street and Stillwell Avenue; service in the northern section bypassed 49th Street in both directions. On September 17, N service was completely suspended and was replaced by W trains in Queens, by Q and W trains in Manhattan, and by [M](/wiki/M_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "M (New York City Subway service)") trains in Brooklyn.[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41) Normal N service was restored on October 28.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

On September 8, 2002, because of the ongoing reconstruction of Coney Island terminal, weekend and late night N service was reduced to a shuttle between 86th and Pacific Streets, running express on the [BMT Fourth Avenue Line](/wiki/BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line "BMT Fourth Avenue Line"). In its place, the [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") was extended to Manhattan and Astoria, Queens at all times; this was because the W was the only route still serving Stillwell Avenue during this part of the reconstruction.[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)[[41]](#cite_note-43)[[42]](#cite_note-44)

On February 22, 2004, the Manhattan Bridge work was finally completed. The N returned to its full route in Manhattan and Queens at all times, and returned to using the Manhattan Bridge at all times except nights (via Fourth Avenue express, bypassing [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "DeKalb Avenue (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)")). On weekdays, N trains ran express between [34th Street](/wiki/34th_Street%E2%80%93Herald_Square_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "34th Street–Herald Square (BMT Broadway Line)") in Manhattan and [59th Street/Fourth Avenue](/wiki/59th_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "59th Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") in Brooklyn via the Manhattan Bridge, and local elsewhere; several trains ran express on the entire Broadway Line and short-turned at [57th Street–Seventh Avenue](/wiki/57th_Street%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "57th Street–Seventh Avenue (BMT Broadway Line)") or Times Square during the morning rush hour. On weekends, it made local stops in Manhattan, but ran express in Brooklyn, using the Bridge. During late nights, it ran local along its entire route via the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel"), replacing the [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") train.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)[[43]](#cite_note-45)[[44]](#cite_note-46)

On May 29, 2005, the new Stillwell Avenue terminal was completed, and N service between 86th Street and Coney Island was restored.[[9]](#cite_note-erictb.info-10)[[45]](#cite_note-47)

On June 28, 2010, the N began running local in Manhattan north of [Canal Street](/wiki/Canal_Street_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "Canal Street (BMT Broadway Line)") at all times, replacing the [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") on weekdays which was discontinued due to budget problems, effectively adopting the weekend service pattern.[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49) However, the handful of short-turn N trains continued to run express in Manhattan.[[48]](#cite_note-50)[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53)

### 2011–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=N_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2011–present")]

From August 2, 2013, to September 14, 2014, the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel") was closed for [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy")–related repairs. During this time, overnight N service was rerouted via the Manhattan Bridge, skipping six stations it normally served.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[52]](#cite_note-54)

On November 7, 2016, the MTA restored the [BMT Broadway Line](/wiki/BMT_Broadway_Line "BMT Broadway Line") services to their 2004–2010 service pattern in preparation for the rerouting of the [Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") train to the [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway"). As a result, the N train once again became a weekday express between 34th Street–Herald Square and Canal Street, with local service replaced by the restored W train.[[53]](#cite_note-:02-55)[[54]](#cite_note-:1-56)[[55]](#cite_note-:2-57) The MTA approved the service change on May 23, 2016.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59) All short-turn N trains that originally terminated at 57th Street–Seventh Avenue were extended to [96th Street–Second Avenue](/wiki/96th_Street_(Second_Avenue_Subway) "96th Street (Second Avenue Subway)") on January 3, 2017, following the [opening](/wiki/Opening_of_the_Second_Avenue_Subway "Opening of the Second Avenue Subway") of the [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway").[[58]](#cite_note-pa-20160726-60)[[59]](#cite_note-N_Timetable-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62)[[61]](#cite_note-63)

In January 2017, the MTA revealed plans to rehabilitate the tunnel structure above the BMT Fourth Avenue Line's express tracks between [36th Street](/wiki/36th_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "36th Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") and [59th Street](/wiki/59th_Street_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "59th Street (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)"). As a result, from July 30, 2018, to July 29, 2019, N trains ran local along that section at all times.[[62]](#cite_note-64)[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)[[65]](#cite_note-67) Between October 21, 2019, and May 4, 2020, N trains terminated at 86th Street so work could be completed to protect Coney Island Yard from flooding. An out-of-system transfer was available between the N at 86th Street and the F at [Avenue X station](/wiki/Avenue_X_station "Avenue X station").[[66]](#cite_note-ciy-68)[[67]](#cite_note-69)

The N began running local in Manhattan in March 2020 after the W was temporarily suspended due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic");[[68]](#cite_note-70) regular N and W service was restored in June.[[69]](#cite_note-71)[[70]](#cite_note-72) In May 2020, the N began operating local along Fourth Avenue on most weekends to provide a transfer to the F at Fourth Avenue–Ninth Street, since the F was suspended south of [Church Avenue](/wiki/Church_Avenue_(IND_Culver_Line) "Church Avenue (IND Culver Line)") due to construction on the [IND Culver Line](/wiki/IND_Culver_Line "IND Culver Line").[[71]](#cite_note-73) In August 2023, midday headways on the N were reduced to eight minutes.[[72]](#cite_note-Simko-Bednarski_2023_u268-74)[[73]](#cite_note-ABC7_New_York_2023_z657-75)

## Route

### Signage history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=N_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Signage history")]

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=N_(New_York_City_Subway_service)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-manhattan-bridge-night_8-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-manhattan-bridge-night_8-1) One late-night N train travels north via the Manhattan Bridge instead, making local stops above Canal Street.[[7]](#cite_note-MBN-7)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-28)** Due to construction on the Manhattan Bridge, the B had been split into two services in 1986: the "orange B" on the Sixth Avenue Line from Herald Square to the Bronx, and the "yellow B" on the Broadway Line from Brooklyn to Ditmars Boulevard.[[26]](#cite_note-27)
3. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-brooklyn-local_80-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-brooklyn-local_80-1) Select rush-hour N trains in the reverse-peak direction provide regular express service in Manhattan but stop at [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "DeKalb Avenue (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") and operate local on the Fourth Avenue Line in Brooklyn.[[75]](#cite_note-4AVS-77)[[76]](#cite_note-4AVN-78) Only one N train with this service pattern provides peak-direction service, roughly an hour before the a.m. rush begins.[[77]](#cite_note-4AVP-79)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-83)** One late-night N train via the Manhattan Bridge stops on the lower level.[[7]](#cite_note-MBN-7)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-84)** Eight trains in the a.m. rush and two trains in the p.m. rush originate from this station; two trains in the a.m. rush terminate here.
